title: Phases and dynamics of an impurity immersed in one-dimensional quantum droplets
authors: Dimitrios Diplaris, Ilias A. Englezos, Friethjof Theel, Peter Schmelcher, Simeon I. Mistakidis
abstract: We explore the ground-state properties of a single impurity immersed in a one-dimensional quantum droplet medium formed by a two-component Bose mixture. Relying on ab-initio simulations, we demonstrate that tuning the impurity-droplet interactions allows to controllably reshape the droplets density profiles and associated correlation patterns. For attractive impurity-medium couplings, the impurity becomes localized within the droplet which exhibits a density hump at the vicinity of the impurity, while repulsive interactions facilitate their phase-separation. Comparing our many-body results to the appropriate extended Gross-Pitaevskii description, we find adequate agreement for the droplet density profiles, with the effective field approach systematically overestimating impurity localization. Following a release of the external trap, we unveil that the sign and magnitude of the interactions between the impurity and the droplet hosts dictate the response of the three-component setting which experiences expansion unless strongly attractive intercomponent couplings are present. These results corroborate the role and presence of correlations in impurity-droplet mixtures and inspire future investigations on impurity physics for probing droplet configurations.
summary: This paper explores the physics of a single bosonic impurity embedded in a 1D quantum droplet. By tuning the interaction between the impurity and the droplet components, the researchers show that one can control density deformations, induce phase separation, or even stabilize the droplet against expansion. The study highlights the importance of many-body correlations beyond the standard Gross-Pitaevskii description. These findings suggest that impurities could serve as powerful tools for probing the internal structure of quantum droplets.
main_problem: The study investigates how a single impurity affects the ground-state properties, phase separation, and expansion dynamics of a one-dimensional two-component quantum droplet.
main_result: The impurity can selectively deform the droplet density profiles, causing density humps for attractive interactions and phase separation for repulsive interactions; furthermore, strong attractive interactions can stabilize the system against expansion following trap release.
method: The authors use the ab-initio Multi-layer Multi-Configuration Time-Dependent Hartree method for Bosons (ML-MCTDHB) and compare it with the extended Gross-Pitaevskii equation (eGPE) and mean-field approximations.
model_or_system: A 1D three-component bosonic mixture consisting of a two-component quantum droplet (species A and B) and a single impurity (species C) in a harmonic trap.
key_observables: One-body density profiles, two-body coherence functions (G2), impurity localization, density overlap parameter, and fidelity/impurity dressing.
important_parameters: Impurity-droplet coupling strengths (gAC, gBC), interaction signs (attractive vs. repulsive), and the LHY fluid regime.
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the 1D regime via strong transverse confinement; the eGPE approach assumes the validity of the Lee-Huang-Yang correction within the local density approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows ground-state densities for non-interacting cases; Figure 2 demonstrates density humps and dips across different coupling strengths; Figure 3 compares density overlap using different models; Figure 4 shows G2 correlation patterns; Figure 7 illustrates expansion dynamics following trap release; Figure 8 shows density profiles in a box potential.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system, details the many-body and effective field methods, analyzes ground-state density deformations and correlation patterns, explores phase separation regimes, investigates expansion dynamics after trap release, and concludes with implications for impurity-based probing of droplets.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into how impurities can be used as local probes to manipulate and sense the structure of self-bound quantum droplets, bridging impurity physics with the study of quantum fluids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Ground-state density distributions of a sym- metric two-component bosonic droplet and an impurity (see legends), at the decoupled limit (i.e. gAC = gBC = 0). The droplet density profile, ρ(1) A (x) = ρ(1) B (x), exhibits a flat-top spatial configuration, while the impurity, ρ(1) C (x), has a Gaus- sian profile localized around the trap center, x = 0. (b) In- traspecies two-body coherence function, G(2) AA(xA 1 , xA 1 ), of the droplet component A. The characteristic droplet correlation pattern characterized by anti-bunching (bunching) across the diagonal (off-diagonal) occurs for the droplet (namely ma- jority) components. The remaining system parameters refer to gAA = gBB =...

Figure 2. Ground-state density profiles for (a) the major- ity species, ρ(1) A (x) = ρ(1) B (x), and (b) the impurity, ρ(1) C (x), for different values of the symmetric impurity-bath coupling gAC = gBC ≡g (see legend). Attractive impurity-bath cou- pling (g < 0) leads to localization of the impurity at the trap center, while being fully trapped by the droplet hosts. For repulsive couplings (g > 0) the impurity distribution broad- ens and eventually phase separates from the droplet hosts. The remaining system parameters are the same to the ones in Fig. 1.

Figure 3. Density overlap, Λ(g), between the majority components and the impurity (see main text) with respect to the symmetric impurity-droplet coupling gAC = gBC ≡ g ∈[0.03, 0.05] within the mean-field (dotted line), the eGPE (dashed line), and the many-body (dash-dotted line) ap- proaches. Evidently, correlation effects significantly impact the transition toward the impurity-droplet phase separation regime. The LHY correction overestimates the degree of at- tractive correlations as compared to the full many-body ap- proach. Other system parameters are the same as in Fig. 1.

Figure 4. Two-body coherence functions in the ground-state of the three-component system featuring attractive and symmetric impurity-droplet interactions of strength gAC = gBC ≡g = −0.05. (a) Intraspecies two-body coherence function, G(2) AA(xA 1 , xA 2 ), of the majority A component. Interspecies two-body coherence function, G(2) σσ′(xσ 1, xσ′ 2 ), (b) between the two majority (droplet) components, σ = A and σ′ = B, and (c) among the majority component σ = A and the impurity σ = C. The characteristic correlation pattern of the droplet (panel (a)) featuring anti-bunching across its core and bunching between its sides is maintained in the presence of the impurity. In contrast,...

Figure 5. (a)-(d) Density distributions of the individual species of the three-component setting for various mixed couplings among the impurity and the two different majority components, namely gAC ̸= gBC (see legend), obtained within the many- body method. For strong attractive-repulsive mixed couplings the impurity imprints a hump-dip alternating structure in the majority components (panel (b)), which transitions to nearly flat-top configurations toward the more weakly coupled impurity- bath case (panel (c)). Eventually, phase-separation occurs when the impurity is decoupled with one majority component and experiences repulsion with the other (panel (d)). (e)-(h) Comparison of the density...

Figure 6. Two-body intracomponent coherence functions of the majority (a), (c) A and (b), (d) B components for mixed impurity-bath couplings (a), (b) gAC = −0.5, gBC = −0.1 and (c), (d) gAC = −0.5, gBC = 0.5. A transition from bunching-to-anti-bunching across the diagonal of the coher- ence of component A occurs, and hence the characteristic droplet correlation pattern is lost due to the impurity. Simi- larly, tuning the interaction of component B and the impurity from attractive to strongly repulsive leads to a modification of the intracomponent coherence pattern for the B species as can be seen by inspecting its diagonal. The remaining param- eters are as in Fig. 1.

Figure 7. Time-evolution of the σ = A, B, C species one-body density, ρ(1) σ (x, t), within the eGPE approach, upon releasing the harmonic trap at t = 0 from ω = 0.005 to ω = 0. The considered settings are characterized by (a)–(c) (gAB, gAC, gBC) = (−0.02, −0.5, 0.5), (d)–(f) (gAB, gAC, gBC) = (−0.02, −0.05, 0.08), (g)–(i) (gAB, gAC, gBC) = (−0.02, 0.0, 0.1), and (j)-(l) (gAB, gAC, gBC) = (−0.1, −0.05, −0.08). In all cases, the first (second) column illustrates the dynamics of the majority compo- nent A (B), and the third visualizes the impurity species C. Apparently, for mixed strong attractive-repulsive impurity-bath interactions (panels (a)-(c)), the majority component that is...

Figure 8. Ground-state density profiles of the different species, ρ(1) σ (x), within the eGPE approach in the absence of the harmonic trap, ω = 0. The impurity species σ = C is (a) weakly and (b) strongly coupled (see legends) with the majority species σ = A, B. The hosts (σ = A, B) configure in flat-top structures featuring a hump (dip) for attractive (re- pulsive) impurity-droplet couplings. The impact of the impu- rity is more prominent as compared to the trapped setting, see also Fig. 5(a), (c). Other system parameters are the same as in Fig. 1.

Figure 9. Fidelity, F(g), of the impurity’s ground-state wave function between the decoupled (g = 0) and symmetrically coupled impurity to the majority components. The fidelity is calculated within the eGPE and the mean-field approxi- mations (see legend) in the presence of a harmonic trap. It is evident that F(g) deviates from unity for g ̸= 0 manifest- ing the impurity’s dressing by the majority components. A more prominent decay takes place for g > 0 due to the phase separation of the impurity with the majority species. The remaining system parameters are the same with the ones of Fig. 1.
